text: Semi-rigid base materials will continue to serve as a dominant base layer in the pavement structures of highways and general roads in our country. However, due to the combined effects of raw material selection, mix ratio design, construction control, and environmental factors, semi-rigid base materials exhibit various defects that severely affect the pavement's performance. On the other hand, with the rapid development of urbanization and infrastructure construction in our country, the generation of construction waste has reached astonishing levels, leading to an increasing conflict between the accumulation of construction waste and the environment. In light of the current situation of substantial construction waste accumulation, the high requirements for base materials in new road constructions, and the significant demand for cement, this research systematically proposes, based on the Xi'an North Ring Expressway project, the idea of finely grinding waste clay bricks and incorporating them with other industrial by-products to formulate construction waste composite powder materials. This study investigates the application of construction waste composite powder materials in semi-rigid base materials, which holds significant theoretical and engineering application value and aligns with our country's sustainable development strategy. The paper first addresses the classification and technical characteristics of construction waste, analyzing its applicability in semi-rigid base materials. It examines the influence of construction waste composite powder material content on the mechanical properties, shrinkage characteristics, and frost resistance of cement-stabilized gravel materials, and delves into the underlying mechanisms affecting these properties, recommending optimal content based on mechanical and durability performance. Experimental results indicate that the main mechanical properties of cement-stabilized gravel containing construction waste composite powder material meet the load-bearing requirements for base layers in road engineering, while the incorporation of such materials improves the shrinkage characteristics and frost resistance of cement-stabilized gravel. Therefore, substituting part of the cement with construction waste composite powder materials in cement-stabilized gravel base materials is technically feasible. Based on the Xi'an North Ring Expressway project, the indoor research findings were tested through a trial pavement construction, indicating the technical feasibility of cement-stabilized gravel with added construction waste composite powder materials. An economic and social benefit analysis of the cement-stabilized gravel incorporating construction waste composite powder materials shows that this incorporation can reduce project costs, lessen land occupation due to construction waste accumulation, and mitigate environmental pollution caused by cement production, yielding significant economic, environmental, and social benefits. Thus, the research findings have a positive significance in enhancing the durability of cement-stabilized gravel materials, fully utilizing construction waste, and alleviating environmental damage caused by construction waste accumulation.

Since ancient times, our country has been a traditionally agricultural nation, and farmers have always been the foundation of our state. Therefore, great importance has been placed on the "three rural issues." Since 2004, the Central Committee of the Communist Party has continuously focused on the development of these issues through its annual "No. 1 Document." However, with the ongoing urbanization of rural areas, the excessive emphasis on economic development at the expense of environmental protection has led to severe pollution in rural environments. This necessitates the correct handling of the relationship between government management and enterprise profit, as well as between economic development and environmental protection. 

Rural waste management is an important topic in rural development, and its efficiency directly affects the progress of constructing a new countryside. However, after reviewing literature and conducting field research, it has been found that in most rural areas, the casual accumulation of garbage and the indiscriminate discharge of wastewater is very common. Effectively addressing rural environmental pollution and waste management poses a significant challenge for local governments. This article utilizes the "polycentric governance" theory by Elinor Ostrom and her husband as the theoretical framework for research. It suggests establishing multiple power centers and governance organizations in rural waste management to enhance efficiency. 

The article analyzes the roles and functions of the government, market, and citizens from the perspective of polycentric governance, thus assessing how they can exert their functional roles in rural waste management. The first section elaborates on related theories and concepts, introducing polycentric governance, public goods theory, and concepts related to rural waste management, expressing their significance for managing rural waste. The second section provides a general description of the geographic and waste management status of County Q. The third section conducts a detailed study of the waste management situation in ten townships of County Q from the perspectives of the government, enterprises, and villagers through surveys and interviews, addressing issues such as the overall cleanliness of towns, the performance of cleaning companies, the accountability of cleaning station managers, the quality of cleaning in key areas, deep burial management, waste bin cleanliness, villagers’ overall attitudes toward waste management, and compliance of cleaning company employees. 

The fourth section proposes pathways to establish a polycentric governance system for rural waste management. Specifically, it emphasizes the need for government-led waste management services, such as innovating rural management systems, establishing grassroots environmental protection agencies, continuously introducing market-oriented operations, strengthening government oversight, improving environmental laws and regulations, and enhancing environmental awareness education among villagers. It also highlights the important role of market mechanisms in rural waste management and strengthens villagers' awareness of their participation. 

In conclusion, rural waste management is a comprehensive governance task that requires a high degree of coordination and order. Based on the case study of County Q, this article emphasizes establishing a supply model with multiple stakeholders and vertical communication. Nonetheless, the practical operation of waste management also involves various factors such as management costs, enterprise profitability, and infrastructure. Therefore, when leveraging the roles of enterprises and the public, the government needs to coordinate among various departments and fully mobilize their enthusiasm, thereby creating a relatively complete "government-enterprise-villager" tripartite cooperation model.

In recent years, due to economic development and the increasing demand for urbanization, the construction-related industries have rapidly expanded. This growth has led to a significant increase in the generation of construction waste, making real-time monitoring and precise management of construction waste piles critically important. Currently, the detection of changes in construction waste piles primarily focuses on two-dimensional spatial variations, neglecting the impact of elevation changes. Furthermore, the volumetric calculation of construction waste piles generally targets individual piles, resulting in cumbersome calculations with low efficiency, and insufficient attention to the risks of safety instability in construction waste. This study, considering the complexity of the three-dimensional variation of construction waste piles, utilizes Resource No. 3 satellite imagery, Phantom 4 RTK aerial survey images, and Focus3D point cloud data, focusing on the study area of Pingdingshan City, Henan Province. It employs three types of data to conduct three-dimensional variation detection of construction waste piles at different scales, with the research content mainly encompassing the following aspects: 

(1) Using stereo images captured by Resource No. 3's multi-line array camera to extract and detect changes in construction waste piles at a local urban scale. Based on the multi-view characteristics of Resource No. 3, a Digital Surface Model (DSM) of the study area is created, and construction waste and buildings are extracted using an elevation feature-based classification method. Additionally, adaptive roughness is used to further delineate the construction waste pile area, with adaptive threshold methods applied to ascertain the range of elevation changes in construction waste piles. Experimental data validation shows that the extraction accuracy for construction waste piles can reach 77.57% in area.

(2) Based on aerial drone photogrammetry data, extraction and three-dimensional variation detection of piles are conducted at the scale of the construction waste disposal site. Upon generating DSM and Digital Orthophoto Map (DOM) for the experimental area, an integration of supervised classification and adaptive roughness methods is employed to extract construction waste piles, analyzing the major causes of three-dimensional variations in construction waste piles. Experimental validation indicates that the extraction accuracy can reach 80.6%.

(3) By employing a ground-to-air fusion method to obtain point cloud data of individual construction waste piles, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and the K-D tree-based Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm are utilized for data fusion, with precision verification conducted. Hierarchical Analysis is used to perform stability analysis of construction waste piles using drone photogrammetry data combined with ground point cloud data. The analysis results indicate that instability risk analysis is informative. Experimental outcomes demonstrate that using satellite remote sensing, aerial drones, and terrestrial three-dimensional laser scanning—combined with elevation feature classification methods and approaches like supervised classification and adaptive roughness—enables the extraction of three-dimensional characteristics of construction waste piles. 

In this paper, surface roughness is introduced in the extraction of construction waste, combined with roughness coefficients using slope length as a parameter to form adaptive roughness. Experimental results indicate that adaptive roughness can specifically extract construction waste piles based on elevation texture from multi-source remote sensing data. Results from the three-dimensional variation detection of construction waste piles show that employing a parameter-based discrete integral method to calculate volumetric and other three-dimensional parameters can effectively acquire volume information of construction waste. Hierarchical analysis is used for instability research of construction waste piles, facilitating predictions of stability risk of the piles. Based on the acquisition of multi-source data, PCA and the K-D tree-based ICP algorithm are used for data fusion, yielding results that meet model accuracy requirements. Experimental findings demonstrate that fused data can more accurately and comprehensively represent the three-dimensional model of construction waste piles. Thus, it is confirmed that utilizing multi-source remote sensing data for monitoring construction waste piles can effectively achieve three-dimensional variation detection of spatial distribution, elevation, and volume characteristics at various scales, and instability analysis of construction waste piles can be conducted based on three-dimensional models, thereby monitoring the risk of instability in construction waste piles. This provides technical support and a data foundation for precise management of construction waste.

Construction waste, as an inevitable solid waste generated during urban construction, poses a series of environmental pollution and social issues that severely constrain the advancement of national circular economy and sustainable development strategies. Therefore, it is crucial to study the characteristic information of construction waste and to quickly and accurately identify its spatial distribution. The self-developed JL1-01A optical remote sensing satellite image from China boasts sub-meter high spatial resolution and a revisit period of just 3.3 days, providing a near real-time reflection of micro changes on the earth's surface, thus offering new avenues for identifying the spatial distribution and change detection of construction waste. However, the unique composition and irregular accumulation boundaries of construction waste lead to extraordinarily complex spectral and geometric characteristics in remote sensing imagery, making it difficult to distinguish from surrounding features. Additionally, the rapid changes and random distribution of accumulation sites present significant challenges for the application of remote sensing technology in the rapid high-precision identification and detection of construction waste, becoming a current research hotspot and difficulty.

This paper focuses on the current high cost and low efficiency associated with traditional methods of extracting construction waste information. It conducts in-depth research by incorporating the characteristics of JL1-01A high-resolution satellite imagery, feature information of construction waste, and extraction methods in remote sensing imagery. Based on the analysis of JL1-01A remote sensing data characteristics, and utilizing the rich spectral, texture, and geometric features of construction waste along with its unique accumulation morphology and the surrounding physical environment, a preprocessing method that incorporates genuine near-infrared information from GF-1 imagery is proposed to compensate for the simulation of the near-infrared band in JL1-01A. Subsequently, the Relief F and J-M distance combination model algorithm is used to select suitable spectral, geometric, and texture features. A multi-feature object-oriented image classification method is employed to establish a knowledge rule model for construction waste and extract its accumulation range. Finally, a method using the fusion of CVA and SGD achieves rapid change detection of construction waste, while a confusion matrix is constructed to evaluate accuracy based on the experimental results.

The main contributions of this study are as follows: (1) To address issues regarding the simulated nature of the near-infrared band in JL1-01A remote sensing imagery affecting target recognition and information extraction of construction waste, a preprocessing method is introduced that incorporates genuine GF-1 near-infrared band information to enhance the band specification of the JL1-01A satellite imagery. (2) To improve the efficiency and accuracy of construction waste identification and extraction, as well as to mitigate the subjectivity in manually selecting multiple features, the research utilizes the technical advantages of multi-feature integration and object-oriented analysis methods to construct a knowledge rule classification model, employing multi-scale segmentation techniques to acquire different hierarchical ground object classes. The Relief F-J-M combination model algorithm enables the optimal selection of construction waste spectral, texture, and geometric features, subsequently using an optimized feature space and rule-based object-oriented classification model to realize multi-feature remote sensing information extraction specific to construction waste from JL1-01A imagery. (3) To further validate the effectiveness of applying JL1-01A imagery to urban fine feature extraction, the necessity of utilizing genuine near-infrared bands from other images, and the rationale behind constructing an optimized feature space for construction waste, extraction experiments were conducted in a typical construction waste accumulation area in Daxing District, Beijing. The precision evaluation of construction waste classification results before and after band information optimization is performed via field validation data and confusion matrix establishment. The results indicate that the proposed feature selection and extraction method can rapidly achieve optimized characteristics for construction waste, with overall classification accuracy reaching 87.6% and 91.3%. Specifically, using the original JL1-01A fused imagery for object-oriented construction waste classification results in an accuracy of 87.6%, which improves to 91.3% with the introduction of genuine near-infrared band information. (4) Building on the knowledge of construction waste spatial distribution, this study investigates a change detection algorithm for construction waste using JL1-01A remote sensing imagery and explores threshold segmentation algorithms that yield optimal change detection results. A change detection framework combining Change Vector Analysis (CVA) and Spectral Gradient Difference (SGD) is developed. Experimental results demonstrate that the CVA-SGD-based construction waste change detection algorithm effectively identifies changes in construction waste; each of the three threshold segmentation algorithms possesses distinct advantages. Among them, the EM algorithm outperforms the other two threshold segmentation techniques, achieving a precision exceeding 90%. Increasing the adjustment factor enhances accuracy, with a precision of 90.28% at an adjustment factor of 0.2 and 90.34% at an adjustment factor of 0.8. The adaptive threshold method yields the lowest false detection rate at just 12.45%, while the global threshold method provides the lowest overall accuracy. This research represents an important exploration into using remote sensing algorithms for construction waste change detection, significantly aiding management agencies in monitoring changes, and demonstrating substantial applied value for the extraction of urban construction waste and other fine feature information from domestic JL1 satellite data.

With the continuous advancement of urbanization, the number of building demolition projects is increasing, resulting in thousands of tons of construction waste. The indiscriminate accumulation of this waste not only leads to significant land resource wastage and environmental pollution but also affects public health. Therefore, the proper disposal of construction waste has become a significant challenge for sustainable urban development. The most common method for handling construction waste is landfilling; however, the establishment of construction waste landfills can impact the surrounding environment, and inappropriate site selection may exacerbate environmental harm, making rational site selection crucial. 

A study of the current state of construction waste management and landfill site selection in China reveals that academic research on construction waste landfill site selection is limited, primarily focusing on municipal solid waste and other solid wastes. Moreover, existing studies on construction waste landfill site selection often consider incomplete site selection factors and employ single-method approaches. This paper, after determining the research methodology, focuses on Changping District of Beijing as the study area. It comprehensively collects data on terrain, climate, geology, and land use planning from various sources, including relevant research materials and publicly available official information. 

Subsequently, it reviews the laws and regulations related to landfill site selection and extracts factors influencing site selection, including geological conditions, natural conditions, hydrological conditions, transportation conditions, and resource conditions. Based on this foundation, using GIS software's buffer analysis and overlay analysis tools, combined with attribute analysis, five preliminary sites are identified. Among the preliminary proposals, a grey relational decision-making method is employed for further suitability analysis, quantifying each influencing factor to determine the optimal decision result as Proposal 5—near Niu Village in Liucun Town and Yangfang Village in Yangfang Town. 

This study constructs a preliminary selection and optimization model for construction waste landfill sites, comprehensively considering various influencing site selection factors, addressing the issues of incomplete factors and singular methods. Focusing on Changping District of Beijing, it identifies the optimal decision results for landfill site selection in this area, demonstrating the feasibility of this approach and providing scientific references for future landfill site selection of construction waste in other domestic cities.

China's pastoral areas cover extensive regions and hold significant importance in the rural revitalization strategy. Improving the living environment is a crucial initiative within this strategy; however, despite the government's high attention, challenges remain in the broad pastoral regions. Most academic research on current waste management issues is based on practices in agricultural villages, neglecting the distinct characteristics, mechanisms, and causes of environmental pollution in rural and pastoral areas. Whether the policies, models, and research outcomes from agricultural village waste management are equally applicable to pastoral practices remains to be discussed. Therefore, this study takes Y Village in Inner Mongolia as a case study to analyze the changes in herders' living modes and their impact on the environment, employing the "Environmental Lifeism" theory to delve into the causes of the "waste surrounding villages" issue, resulting in the following conclusions: (1) As living modes change, the relationships between production, living, and ecological spaces also evolve. In traditional periods, herders established a reasonable ecological living space through long-term interactions with the grasslands. After encirclement and settlement, their living space shrinks, competition for productive and living space intensifies, and ecological areas are encroached upon, leading to the predicament of "waste surrounding villages." (2) The transformation of living modes has caused a fracture in herders' social interaction networks, facilitating the occurrence of the "waste surrounding villages" phenomenon. In densely populated settlement villages, the modes of interaction and ecological knowledge developed during traditional periods are ineffective in adapting to current environments. However, new social interaction networks have yet to be established, preventing herders from realizing the importance of resource sharing and mutual maintenance of public spaces. (3) The shift in living modes redefines the values of herders through three dimensions: cultural identity, economic interests, and knowledge levels. This transformation significantly affects herders' cognitive attitudes towards the environment and their behavioral patterns of environmental interaction, thus exacerbating the prominence of the "waste surrounding villages" issue. (4) Y Village also has notable problems regarding waste management infrastructure, which serve as external causes for the "waste surrounding villages" phenomenon. Specifically, the location layout of waste collection points is unreasonable, the incineration facilities have not achieved expected outcomes, and there is a lack of projects for the recovery and utilization of manure, collectively resulting in insufficient waste treatment and recycling efforts. To address the "waste surrounding villages" issue, grassroots governments, village committees, and herders have collaboratively undertaken various actions with initial success. However, overcoming the "waste surrounding villages" phenomenon in Y Village and building a beautiful and livable pastoral village still faces multiple difficulties. In light of this, this study represents an in-depth exploration of waste management challenges in pastoral areas and is an active attempt to apply the "Environmental Lifeism" theory to pastoral practices. Through this research, we aim to provide innovative ideas and methods for managing household waste in pastoral areas, thereby promoting the continuous optimization and sustainable development of the pastoral ecological environment.

[Objective] To study the rainfall erosion process and mechanisms of garbage accumulation in the Weibei dry plateau, providing a scientific basis for the prevention and control of rainfall erosion in garbage piles in the Weibei dry plateau. [Methods] Based on artificial simulated rainfall experiments, two rainfall intensities (1.2 and 2.0 mm/min) and two garbage accumulation types (i.e., garbage piles sloped and garbage piles on a non-eroding surface) were designed for a comparative study of rainfall erosion processes between the two types of garbage accumulation and exposed abandoned slopes. [Results] At a rainfall intensity of I=1.2 mm/min, the total sediment yield from the sloped garbage accumulation was 117.94 kg, while the sediment yield from the abandoned slope was 10.86 kg, indicating that the sloped garbage accumulation produced 107.08 kg more sediment than the abandoned slope; at a rainfall intensity of I=2.0 mm/min, the sloped garbage accumulation yielded 225.72 kg more sediment than the abandoned slope, whereas the erosion sediment yield from the garbage pile on the non-eroding surface was relatively low. Analysis of the erosion energy (E) of runoff on the garbage accumulation slope and the energy consumed in erosion (E initiating sediment, E transporting sediment), along with the drag force (F) exerted by the sediment-laden flow on the bed, revealed that the garbage accumulation and the underlying surface formed gaps or weak zones, resulting in runoff on the slope that reduced the energy consumed to mobilize loose sediment particles in the garbage accumulation. The erosion energy (E) from the slope runoff was mainly expended in transporting the garbage accumulation particles. [Conclusion] Water flows containing garbage particles will maintain a high flow rate, and the strong drag force generated by these flows exacerbates rill erosion.

Based on the morphological forms of waste accumulation resulting from indiscriminate dumping on various original landforms, four types of waste accumulation are categorized through field investigations: (1) Newly Formed Slope Accumulation Type. Waste is dumped on a smooth surface free from erosion, leading to the formation of erosion landforms, characteristic of the Longgang low hill waste accumulation morphology. This type primarily occurs on original landforms such as high-level land, alluvial plains, and river floodplains. (2) Newly Formed Steep Slope Accumulation Type. Waste is dumped on slopes, naturally sliding down, which causes the original slope gradient to increase to between 24° and 38°. The slope becomes steeper, approximately equal to the angle of repose of the soil and rock. This type mainly occurs on both riverbanks, hillside regions, and at the foot of slopes where sheet-like waste accumulations form at mid-slope levels. (3) Newly Formed Plateau Accumulation Type. Loose waste is consistently stacked around the leveled tops of ridges, spurs, and hills to expand the area of the leveled surfaces, resulting in a plateau-like waste accumulation landform. (4) Low-lying Area Superimposed Accumulation Type. Waste is dumped in gullies or lower terrains, leading to long-term accumulation that fills in some concave surfaces. Gullies, ravines, and concave slopes act as pathways for water collection and drainage, creating a weak interface between the original substrate and the loose deposits formed by waste. This weak interface becomes susceptible to erosion due to rainfall runoff. These four types of waste accumulation landforms have emerged as unstable surface forms due to local villagers' arbitrary dumping of domestic waste in the gully region of the Weibei Loess Plateau. With the continuous increase in population and improvement in living standards, the volume of production waste has also risen. The indiscriminate dumping of waste without management poses severe health risks to the local population. Additionally, the loose accumulations of waste are highly susceptible to the impacts of rainfall and strong winds, leading to severe anthropogenic soil erosion.

Urbanization has facilitated socio-economic development. Due to the high concentration of urban populations and extensive utilization of natural resources, urban areas generate a significant amount of waste during production and consumption processes. Taking Henan Province as an example, the 38 existing cities in Henan produce a total of 5 million tons of municipal solid waste annually. If we calculate based on Henan's urbanization level reaching the current national average of 30%, by 2005, the urban population in Henan is projected to reach 30.47 million. Considering the current per capita waste generation in Zhengzhou of 1.17 kilograms per person per day, it is estimated that by 2005, the annual municipal solid waste produced in cities will amount to 13.1 million tons, with the 38 cities generating 7.78 million tons and towns producing 5.32 million tons. This waste is widely dumped in suburban areas, occupying arable land, causing environmental pollution, and creating a "garbage city" phenomenon. Over the past decade, urban waste in China has increased at an average annual rate of 8.98%, with cities like Beijing experiencing annual growth rates of 15% to 20%. Among nearly 400 large cities nationwide, two-thirds are encircled by waste, accumulating a total of 600 million tons of disposed waste, eroding approximately 500 million square meters of land, and generating new waste at a rate exceeding one million tons per year. A significant amount of urban solid waste, particularly untreated waste, is transported to suburban areas and openly dumped in abandoned ditches, pits, low-lying wastelands, and even arable land. This disorganized dumping leads to small-scale, numerous waste piles. Open dumping not only occupies vast areas of arable land but also, due to the lack of sanitary landfill and proper disposal, results in waste weathering, exposure to sun and rain, decomposition of organic matter, and leachate contaminating soil, groundwater, and surface water. Dust and gases from the waste pollute the atmosphere, exacerbating suburban environmental degradation. The "garbage city" phenomenon has become a significant source of urban environmental pollution.

With the development of the national economy, the scale of modern urban construction has rapidly expanded, and residents' consumption levels continue to rise, resulting in significant amounts of municipal solid waste generated daily and annually in each city. This municipal solid waste, with only a small portion being recycled, is predominantly concentrated and piled for disposal in selected locations around the city. Waste disposal sites are generally chosen in low-lying or flat areas near urban centers. The waste comprises a diverse range of materials, including various organic substances, chemical industrial byproducts, ash, and debris, with extremely complex compositions and significant quantities of toxic and harmful substances. Over time, these sites have accumulated waste, expanding in area and increasing in fill thickness, creating a distinctive anthropogenic "landscape" and site conditions. Municipal solid waste sites not only directly impact urban aesthetics, environmental hygiene, and public health but also present numerous unique adverse geological issues from an engineering perspective.

(1) The sites formed by the accumulation of municipal solid waste, due to their relatively short accumulation period, have complex compositions, high organic matter content, long consolidation periods, and significant compressibility, and often exhibit erosive properties. Therefore, they can significantly adversely affect various aspects of engineering structures. Whether for roads, railways, or industrial and civilian buildings, these areas should be avoided whenever possible. (2) When it is necessary for a route or construction to pass through or be built on a landfill site, priority should be given to measures that involve the removal of municipal solid waste. If the fill thickness is excessive, making removal difficult and costly, reliable treatment methods and measures must be adopted. For critical structures, pile foundations with deeper treatment should be implemented. (3) The key considerations in pile foundation design include: (i) The municipal solid waste fill layer has very low bearing capacity and, as it continues to settle, will generate substantial negative friction. The pile foundation must maintain sufficient strength and depth to meet the foundation requirements of the structure; (ii) Regarding the erosive nature of the fill material, aside from chemical erosion, there are also conditions conducive to biological corrosion on the surfaces of metal objects. Therefore, appropriate anti-corrosion measures must be selected during the design phase, or materials with good erosion resistance, such as high-performance anti-corrosive cement, should be utilized. (4) During construction, special attention should be paid to the effects of toxic and flammable gases. Reliable construction methods and health protection measures must be implemented to prevent harmful gases from posing risks to personnel. Fire safety requirements should be strictly observed, and monitoring instruments should be equipped when necessary to prevent incidents such as combustion and explosion.

With the indiscriminate accumulation or burial of construction waste during urbanization, a significant number of informal construction waste disposal sites have emerged, leading to the phenomenon of "waste encroaching into urban areas," which has become a pressing social issue. Therefore, studying the spatial distribution of construction waste and countermeasures is crucial for the optimized development of construction waste spatial layout in our country. Taking Changping District of Beijing as an example, this research employs Google Earth and GIS software to identify the number, geographical locations, areas of informal construction waste dumping sites, and their distances from residential areas and rivers, thus obtaining waste distribution information. By analyzing the spatial layout characteristics such as point locations, distribution, and elevation of this information, the spatial layout issues of informal waste sites are clarified, with the aim to provide references for the government and relevant departments to enhance the management of construction waste and reduce the risks associated with urban waste encroachment.

This article takes Changping District of Beijing as a case study to analyze the geospatial distribution characteristics of non-construction waste landfills based on GIS and the issues associated with them. The conclusions are as follows: 1) From the spatial point analysis of construction waste sites, it is observed that informal landfills in Changping District are predominantly concentrated in the central region, exhibiting a high quantity and density. The analysis of site quantities reveals an uneven distribution of construction waste sites across different areas, with significant variability. The density distribution results are consistent with the site quantity analysis, indicating that factors such as production, population density, and demolition and renovation activities significantly influence spatial distribution. 2) Regarding the total area of construction waste sites in Changping District, the primary distribution is in the southeastern region, with considerable differences among towns (streets). Among them, Baishan Town has the highest area density, and the identified construction waste sites show varying scales, predominantly surrounding construction facilities and demolition areas. Additionally, urban renewal and waste collection and treatment mechanisms have considerable impacts on construction waste placement. Furthermore, the placement of construction waste is highly correlated with proximity to residential areas and river distribution; landfills located within 500 meters of residential areas account for 89% of the total, and those within 1500 meters of rivers comprise 64.85%. The pollution risk posed by construction waste placement to the environment is notably high. 3) In terms of elevation distribution, construction waste sites are densely concentrated in the southern and southeastern parts, particularly evident in old city demolition areas with a high point density. Most landfills are situated in plain areas with relatively few in mountainous regions, showing a distinct spatial aggregation trend that gradually concentrates toward the gentler areas in the central and southern parts.

Construction waste exhibits complex spatiotemporal characteristics, making its generation and variation difficult to estimate and accurately monitor. Research in the fields of surveying science and environmental science has addressed these issues. The precise identification of multi-source remote sensing of construction waste, along with accurate monitoring and simulation of its volume and variation, is fundamental to enhancing urban ecological environment protection and management, as well as revealing the metabolic mechanisms of urban building systems. From an interdisciplinary perspective, this paper reviews the development trends and representative research advancements concerning the spatial distribution, volume estimation, and change monitoring and simulation of construction waste both domestically and internationally, aiming to further support in-depth studies of environmental protection and urban construction metabolism. The monitoring of construction waste and its spatial distribution is categorized into traditional methods and remote sensing identification methods assisted by machine learning or deep learning. Volume estimation methods for construction waste include statistical data-based estimation and three-dimensional model-based estimation. Lastly, the current state of research on dynamic monitoring of construction waste variation and urban construction metabolism simulation is summarized. By systematically analyzing and summarizing the research achievements in this field, the paper highlights existing issues and prospects for future development, offering valuable insights for improving the regulatory framework of solid wastes such as construction waste, protecting the urban ecological environment, and promoting interdisciplinary research on urban construction metabolism.

This study focuses on a waste accumulation site in a small town in Shandong Province. A total of 17 soil samples were collected from both within the site and its boundary to analyze the content distribution characteristics of 15 inorganic and heavy metal pollutants, along with 195 volatile and semi-volatile organic pollutants. By comparing with various domestic and international soil reference values, the potential ecological risks and health risks associated with detected heavy metals were assessed. The results indicate that the potential ecological risk of heavy metals in the landfill soil is generally at a minor level. However, when using the soil background values of Shandong Province as a reference, the ecological risk level due to Hg accumulation in the landfill soil reaches strong to very strong levels. For all heavy metals, except As, the ecological risk in the landfill soil is higher than that at the boundary. For both children and adults, the carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic risks associated with the landfill soil are within acceptable ranges. The total non-carcinogenic risk among different groups shows little variation, with the heavy metals primarily contributing to adverse health effects being As and Cr, and the main exposure pathways identified are direct oral ingestion and dermal contact.

Chen Zefeng, Vice President of the China Environmental Protection Industry Association, proposed that the annual municipal solid waste generation in China will reach 1.79×10^8 tons and 2.1×10^8 tons in 2015 and 2020, respectively. Given China's reality of a large population and limited land and energy, coupled with rapid urbanization, waste incineration for energy generation is the most prudent choice aligned with national conditions. Data indicates that the accumulated waste in China over the years has exceeded 6.0×10^9 tons, occupying more than 20 hectares and causing severe environmental pollution and even disasters in surrounding areas. Two-thirds of over 600 cities in the country are surrounded by waste. Statistics reveal that the current annual municipal solid waste generation in China is approximately 1.8×10^8 tons, with 90% being landfilled, only 7% incinerated, and the remainder managed through composting and other methods. In 1,636 counties across the nation, the annual waste generation is around 5.0×10^7 tons. Among more than 650 cities in the country, 325 cities still lack municipal waste treatment facilities. A large amount of waste is simply piled up, causing significant odors and carbon monoxide emissions, which seriously pollute water bodies, the atmosphere, and soil. Currently, the main methods for municipal solid waste management in China are sanitary landfilling, biological composting, and waste incineration for power generation. Chen Zefeng pointed out that waste incineration has numerous advantages, such as requiring less space, which helps conserve land resources; achieving a high degree of waste volume reduction; operating in a fully enclosed, around-the-clock manner that ensures a high level of environmental protection; and the waste incineration residual heat can generate steam for electricity and heating, conserving energy in line with China’s energy-saving and emission reduction strategy. If the country were to completely incinerate its waste for energy recovery, it could save over 3.6×10^7 tons of coal annually, significantly reduce potential safety incidents related to coal mining, and lower carbon dioxide emissions by more than 9.4×10^7 tons, thus suppressing global warming and contributing to China's energy conservation, emission reduction, and low-carbon economy initiatives.

Village appearance improvement is an essential aspect of the construction of a new socialist countryside. Effective rural waste management and enhancement of rural sanitation are fundamental components of this process. However, due to a long-term absence of responsible management entities and severe lack of treatment facilities, an effective rural domestic waste pollution prevention and control system has yet to be established. Hence, developing a robust system for managing rural domestic waste has become a subject worthy of study. This paper aims to investigate the issues present in rural domestic waste management and explore innovative management mechanisms and models. Focusing on the status of rural waste management in Cengxi City, and utilizing theories of public goods and collaborative governance, combined with practices in rural governance and environmental management in China, a comprehensive approach employing literature research, normative analysis, field surveys, and case studies will be adopted to conduct a systematic and in-depth examination of various links and key elements in the rural domestic waste management process in Cengxi City. 

The article primarily analyzes the challenges faced in rural domestic waste governance from the perspectives of institutional supply, public goods provision, waste treatment methods and management models, and villagers' environmental awareness. It unpacks the causes of these challenges and, based on this analysis, draws on experiences from South Korea and Germany to propose solutions, including the establishment and improvement of legal regulations and institutional frameworks, enhancing financial investments and public goods supply mechanisms, transforming the omnipotent government governance model, and strengthening environmental awareness and public morals education among villagers. 

Furthermore, it concludes that constructing a collaborative governance model involving the government, autonomous village organizations, and villagers is an effective path for managing rural domestic waste. It points out that the government must adopt a new governance paradigm to streamline its relationship with rural social structures, foster village autonomous organizations, enhance farmers' capacity for participation and collaboration, and facilitate the establishment of a strong relational network and communication mechanism among the three parties. In a situation where the government is evidently unable to shoulder the rural waste management responsibilities alone and rural autonomous organizations have yet to develop robust capabilities, the model of collaborative governance reflects a novel interactive relationship between the government and rural society. This model transcends traditional participatory governance and serves as an alternative to the "government governance, public participation" approach.

Construction waste, also known as construction debris, refers to materials that are generated during the construction, maintenance, and demolition activities of buildings and structures, which are deemed unnecessary or unwanted for the buildings themselves. With the rapid acceleration of urbanization in our country, the volume of urban construction waste is increasing sharply, accounting for approximately 30% to 40% of total urban waste, with an annual total exceeding one hundred million tons, posing a significant obstacle to urban development. In the context of advocating for energy conservation and emission reduction, efforts to minimize the generation of construction waste and promote its recycling are not only essential for urban environmental sanitation work but also a necessary requirement for future sustainable development. This article conducts the following research on construction waste based on a comprehensive review of a large volume of literature and investigative studies. By comparing the current status of research and management practices domestically and internationally, this paper summarizes the shortcomings of current research in our country, draws on foreign management strategies, and analyzes the fundamental theoretical knowledge related to construction waste, providing a detailed definition, composition, characteristics, and environmental hazards of construction waste. It introduces the current technical and economic policies adopted in our country regarding construction waste and compares the legal and regulatory frameworks of the United States, Japan, and our country. Addressing the confusion regarding the volume of construction waste, data from Guangzhou was utilized to calculate its production; principal component analysis determined that the main factors influencing construction waste volume are construction area, demolition amount, renovation volume, and urban population. These variables were used to perform a regression analysis for estimating the construction waste volume in Guangzhou, leading to a formulated estimation equation, and employing time series analysis to predict future volumes. Following the construction waste processing process, this article introduces stepwise processing techniques and equipment, comparing the advantages and disadvantages of various typical processing methodologies and suggesting improvements. Different recycling methods are proposed based on the composition of construction waste, along with a brief economic analysis. Finally, the article investigates related organizations and enterprises involved in construction waste processing in Guangzhou, analyzes the current challenges and policy barriers faced by construction waste processing companies, proposes a construction waste processing industry chain model and a comprehensive management model, and offers recommendations for construction waste management practices in our country. Some viewpoints and recommendations presented in this paper may serve as a reference for relevant government agencies and enterprises in the comprehensive utilization of construction waste, with significant implications for the green ecological construction of the building industry.

With the rapid development of the economy and society and the swift advancement of urbanization in China, the volume of new construction, renovation, and expansion projects, as well as demolition activities, has continuously increased, resulting in a significant generation of construction waste. This poses a tremendous threat to both the social environment and the economy. Therefore, the issue of construction waste is urgent and necessitates resolution; research and analysis on the resource utilization of construction waste is both a requirement of the situation and a compulsion of circumstances. This paper employs the building area estimation method and grey prediction model to estimate and forecast the generation of construction waste in China. Coupled with the current state of construction waste resource utilization domestically and internationally, it identifies issues such as weak environmental protection awareness, insufficient government support, inadequate legal and regulatory frameworks, and imperfect management policies in China's construction waste resource utilization. By integrating theory with practice, this paper proposes a pathway for developing the construction waste resource utilization industry, clarifies the participants in the operation of this industry, establishes an operational model for construction waste resource utilization, and conducts an analysis of it. It delves into the technical and economic management policies required for the operation of the construction waste resource utilization industry and provides a technical exposition of corresponding resource utilization pathways for different types of construction waste, affirming the technological foundation for the development of this industry. Additionally, addressing the challenges faced in construction waste resource utilization in China from a holistic social perspective, the paper proposes strategic recommendations to advance the operation of the construction waste resource utilization industry in the country. Lastly, it analyzes the social, environmental, and economic benefits achieved through the implementation of construction waste resource utilization in China, thereby encouraging enterprises, research institutions, and the general public to participate in and develop construction waste resource utilization. This paper presents novel methods and viewpoints regarding construction waste resource utilization, indicates the direction for the industrial development of construction waste resource utilization in China, and offers an operational model for the industry along with strategic recommendations for its execution, providing systematic and scientific references and decision-making basis for related enterprises, the public, and the government.

As urbanization accelerates, the generation and discharge of construction and demolition waste in cities are rapidly increasing. The management and utilization of this waste have become a significant issue that must be addressed. The resource recovery of construction waste has emerged as an effective method to mitigate the environmental impacts associated with it. To alleviate the pressure caused by construction waste, this paper investigates construction waste resource recovery. Through an investigation of the current status of construction waste management in Xi'an, Shaanxi Province, this study examines measures for resource recovery. The survey results indicate that the region produces a substantial amount of construction waste, which is predominantly disposed of through simplistic methods and has low utilization rates. The large volume of construction waste contributes to environmental pollution and resource wastage, gradually impacting urban development. The primary reasons for the current state of construction waste include the low level of technology for resource recovery, a lack of relevant legal and regulatory frameworks, and insufficient policy support for companies engaged in construction waste resource recovery. This paper employs case analysis to examine existing mature technologies for construction waste resource recovery, integrating the findings with the local conditions of construction waste, economic factors, and management practices in the surveyed area. By referencing successful domestic and international experiences in construction waste resource recovery, it proposes measures to advance the resource recovery of urban construction waste from technical, economic, and management perspectives. These measures consist of four parts: first, promoting the industrialization of construction waste management; second, establishing relevant policy support for construction waste resource recovery; third, implementing comprehensive management of construction waste; and fourth, creating an industrial chain for construction waste resource recovery. The findings of this research can provide theoretical support for the resource recovery of construction waste in Xi'an and other cities across the country, holding significant implications for reducing construction waste and promoting rational utilization.
